PoliticalOrientation: Leftist; Anti-authoritarian; Critical of State and Institutional Power
KeyWorks: Discipline and Punish; The History of Sexuality; The Order of Things; Madness and Civilization

CoreTeachings: Habermas developed a theory of communicative action in which rationality is rooted in the practices of argumentation and mutual understanding rather than in instrumental control. He argued that modern societies differentiate system (economy, administration) from lifeworld (culture, society, personality), warning of the colonization of the lifeworld by systemic imperatives. In ethics and politics, he proposed discourse ethics and deliberative democracy, where norms are justified through inclusive, unconstrained communication among affected participants. His work seeks to reconstruct the project of enlightenment in a more reflexive, communicative form.
HistoricalContext: Born in Düsseldorf, Germany, Habermas grew up under Nazism and came of age in postwar West Germany, where confronting the past was a central intellectual and political task. He studied under figures such as Adorno and worked at the Frankfurt School before holding professorships at various German universities. His career has been marked by engagement in public debates about democracy, technology, bioethics, and European integration.
EpistemologicalStance: Critical Theory; Pragmatic and Linguistic Turn
EthicalOrientation: Discourse Ethics; Universalization via Communication
PoliticalOrientation: Social Democratic; Deliberative Democrat; Pro-European Integration
KeyWorks: The Theory of Communicative Action; Moral Consciousness and Communicative Action; Between Facts and Norms

CoreTeachings: Derrida developed deconstruction as a way of reading texts that reveals internal tensions, undecidable meanings, and the instability of binary oppositions. He argued that meaning is always deferred (différance) and that no interpretation can fully master a text’s play of signification. Critiquing logocentrism and metaphysics of presence, he showed how philosophical and literary works rely on exclusions and hierarchies that can be reversed and displaced. His approach influenced literary theory, philosophy, legal studies, and cultural criticism.
HistoricalContext: Born into a Jewish family in French Algeria, Derrida experienced colonial and anti-Semitic discrimination, including exclusion from school under Vichy laws. He studied at the École Normale Supérieure in Paris and became a central figure in French philosophy and international theory circles from the late 1960s onward. His work emerged in the context of structuralism, postwar phenomenology, and political upheavals, including May 1968.
EthicalOrientation: No Systematic Normative Ethics; Political Engagement in Later Work
PoliticalOrientation: Left-leaning; Anti-apartheid; Pro-democracy
ReligiousOrientation: Jewish background; Secular; Critical of Religious Metaphysics
KeyWorks: Of Grammatology; Writing and Difference; Speech and Phenomena; Limited Inc

CoreTeachings: Deleuze developed a metaphysics of difference and becoming, rejecting identity-based, hierarchical ontologies in favor of multiplicities, flows, and rhizomes. In works with Félix Guattari, he analyzed capitalism, desire, and subjectivity, proposing concepts such as deterritorialization, assemblages, and bodies without organs. He opposed representational thinking, emphasizing creativity, experimentation, and immanence. His philosophy influenced political theory, cultural studies, and art by offering new ways to think about power, subjectivity, and social organization.
HistoricalContext: Born in Paris, Deleuze studied and taught philosophy at several French universities, including Vincennes (Paris VIII), where he worked in an experimental, politically engaged environment after 1968. He engaged critically with the history of philosophy, writing major studies on figures like Hume, Nietzsche, Spinoza, and Bergson. Working in postwar and post-1968 France, he collaborated with psychoanalyst Félix Guattari, drawing on Marx, Freud, and structuralism while transforming them.
EpistemologicalStance: Conceptual Creation; Genealogical and Critical Readings
EthicalOrientation: No Systematic Normative Ethics; Emphasis on Experimentation and Liberation
PoliticalOrientation: Leftist; Anti-authoritarian; Sympathetic to May 1968 Movements
ReligiousOrientation: Raised in a secular French context; Non-religious
KeyWorks: Difference and Repetition; A Thousand Plateaus (with Guattari); Anti-Oedipus (with Guattari); Nietzsche and Philosophy

CoreTeachings: Confucius taught that moral cultivation, ritual propriety (li), and humaneness (ren) are central to personal and political life. He emphasized filial piety, respect for tradition, and the role of exemplary persons (junzi) whose virtue radiates outward to harmonize society. Rather than metaphysical speculation, he focused on ethical practice, proper roles, and governance by moral example rather than coercion. His sayings, preserved in the Analects, became foundational for East Asian ethics and statecraft.
HistoricalContext: Confucius was born in the state of Lu during the late Spring and Autumn period, a time of political fragmentation and social upheaval in ancient China. He worked as a minor official and teacher, traveling among states to offer advice to rulers, though his reforms were not fully adopted in his lifetime. After his death, his disciples preserved and developed his teachings, which later became core to imperial civil service education for centuries.
MetaphysicalStance: Moderate Realism about Moral and Social Order
EpistemologicalStance: Practical Reason; Appeal to Tradition and Exemplars
EthicalOrientation: Virtue Ethics; Role Ethics; Relational Ethics
PoliticalOrientation: Meritocratic Monarchy; Hierarchical but Benevolent Governance
ReligiousOrientation: Ritual-centered; Ancestor Veneration; Not Theistic in a Western Sense
KeyWorks: Analects

CoreTeachings: Mencius argued that human nature is originally good, containing sprouts of virtue such as compassion and a sense of shame that can be cultivated. He emphasized the role of moral emotions and intuitive judgments in ethics, using vivid examples to show our natural responsiveness to others’ suffering. Politically, he defended benevolent government that secures people’s material well-being and moral development, allowing removal of tyrants who violate these duties. His interpretation of Confucianism stressed inner moral resources and popular welfare.
HistoricalContext: Mencius lived during the Warring States period, traveling among rival states as a scholar-adviser who sought to persuade rulers to adopt humane policies. He engaged in debates with other schools such as Mohism and early Legalism, refining Confucian positions in a competitive intellectual environment. His dialogues, preserved in the text Mengzi, became canonical for later Confucian thought in China, Korea, Japan, and Vietnam.
MetaphysicalStance: Optimistic View of Human Nature; Moral Realism
EpistemologicalStance: Intuitionist; Reflective Endorsement of Moral Sprouts
EthicalOrientation: Virtue Ethics; Benevolent Governance
PoliticalOrientation: Advocates Humane Monarchy; Conditional Right to Overthrow Tyrants
KeyWorks: Mengzi (Mencius)
Tags: Human nature is good;Moral sprouts;Benevolent rule

CoreTeachings: Xunzi argued against Mencius that human nature is bad in the sense of spontaneously tending toward selfishness and conflict, requiring transformation through ritual, education, and conscious effort. He emphasized the importance of institutions, norms, and deliberate training to shape desires and behavior. While affirming Confucian values, he leaned toward a more realistic, law-and-order view of society that influenced later Legalism. His work provides a contrasting, more pessimistic strand within early Confucianism.
HistoricalContext: Xunzi lived in the late Warring States period and held official positions in several states, including Qi and Chu, while also running his own school. His students included Han Feizi and Li Si, who became key figures in the development of Legalism and the Qin dynasty. He wrote in prose essays rather than dialogues, synthesizing and systematizing earlier debates about ritual, human nature, and governance.
MetaphysicalStance: Conventionalist View of Value; Emphasis on Ritual and Order
EpistemologicalStance: Rational Reflection; Skepticism toward Spontaneous Morality
EthicalOrientation: Virtue and Rule-based Ethics; Transformative Education
PoliticalOrientation: Supports Strong Hierarchical State; Proto-Legalist Influences
ReligiousOrientation: Confucian; Non-theistic; Emphasis on Ritual over Spirits
KeyWorks: Xunzi
Tags: Human nature is bad;Ritual and education;Bridge to Legalism

CoreTeachings: Laozi, as presented in the Daodejing, advocated living in accordance with the Dao, a mysterious, ineffable source and pattern of the cosmos. He valorized wu-wei (non-forcing or effortless action), simplicity, softness, and humility over striving, competition, and artificial norms. Politically, he favored minimal, unobtrusive rule that lets people live simply and naturally. His thought questions rigid moralism and rigidly planned action, emphasizing spontaneity and returning to an uncarved simplicity.
HistoricalContext: Little is historically certain about Laozi; he is often portrayed as an older contemporary of Confucius, sometimes as an archivist who withdrew from society. The Daodejing likely took shape over time during the late Spring and Autumn and early Warring States periods, reflecting responses to social disorder. The figure of Laozi and the text associated with him became central to philosophical Daoism and later religious Daoist traditions.
EthicalOrientation: Way-centered Ethics; Naturalness and Non-interference
PoliticalOrientation: Minimalist Governance; Quietist and Anti-legalist
ReligiousOrientation: Early Daoist; Later Associated with Religious Daoism
KeyWorks: Daodejing (Tao Te Ching)

CoreTeachings: Zhuangzi used playful stories, paradoxes, and fantastical dialogues to challenge rigid distinctions and dogmatic claims to knowledge. He emphasized spontaneity, wandering beyond conventional norms, and adapting to the transformations of the Dao. Skeptical of fixed perspectives, he suggested that what counts as right or useful depends on context and standpoint. His text celebrates freedom from narrow roles and acceptance of change, influencing later Daoist and Chan/Zen traditions.
HistoricalContext: Zhuangzi lived during the Warring States period, in a time of competing philosophical schools and political turmoil. Biographical details are sparse, but anecdotes depict him refusing lucrative office and choosing a life of independence. The text bearing his name is composite, with Inner Chapters more likely reflecting his thought and later chapters including contributions from various Daoist currents.
MetaphysicalStance: Relativizing, Processual View of Reality; Dao-centered
EthicalOrientation: Spontaneity-centered Ethics; Anti-rigid Norms
PoliticalOrientation: Generally Apolitical or Critical of Officialdom
ReligiousOrientation: Daoist; Irreverent toward Ritual and Orthodoxy
KeyWorks: Zhuangzi (Chuang Tzu)

CoreTeachings: Mozi promoted jian ai, often translated as impartial or inclusive care, arguing that social problems arise from partiality toward one’s own group. He advocated consequentialist reasoning: policies and practices should be judged by how they benefit the people in terms of order, wealth, and population. He opposed elaborate rituals, music, and funerals as wasteful, and criticized offensive warfare. His thought presents an early, rigorously argued alternative to Confucianism, emphasizing utility and impartial concern.
HistoricalContext: Mozi lived during the early Warring States period and led a disciplined community of followers who engaged practically, even militarily, to defend weaker states from aggression. His school functioned almost like a moral and technical corps, advising on fortifications and social policy. Although Mohism later declined and texts were lost, rediscoveries in the modern era have renewed interest in his systematic ethical and political arguments.
MetaphysicalStance: Theistic Heavens with Moral Order; Consequentialist Orientation
EthicalOrientation: Consequentialism; Impartial Care; Frugality
PoliticalOrientation: Meritocratic; Opposed to Aggressive War; Technocratic Tendencies
ReligiousOrientation: Belief in Heaven (Tian) as Moral Authority
KeyWorks: Mozi

CoreTeachings: Han Feizi synthesized Legalist ideas, arguing that effective rule depends on strict laws (fa), administrative techniques (shu), and the position of power (shi), rather than moral virtue. He viewed people as self-interested and advocated clear, impersonal rewards and punishments to align behavior with state goals. Suspicious of scholars and moralists, he emphasized centralized authority and control of information. His thought provided a theoretical basis for the authoritarian unification of China under the Qin dynasty.
HistoricalContext: A prince of the state of Han, Han Feizi studied under Xunzi and wrote during the late Warring States era, when states competed for survival and dominance. His writings attracted the attention of the Qin ruler, but court intrigue led to his imprisonment and forced suicide. Nonetheless, his Legalist doctrines were influential in Qin and early Han governance, even as later Confucian historians portrayed them negatively.
MetaphysicalStance: Political Realism; Pessimistic View of Human Motivation
EpistemologicalStance: Pragmatic, Empirical Assessment of Human Behavior
EthicalOrientation: Instrumental State-centered Ethics
PoliticalOrientation: Authoritarian; Centralized Bureaucratic State; Harsh Penal Law
ReligiousOrientation: Formally Confucian-educated; Politically Secular and Anti-ritualist
KeyWorks: Han Feizi

CoreTeachings: Zhu Xi systematized Neo-Confucian philosophy, integrating metaphysical concepts of principle (li) and vital force (qi) with moral self-cultivation. He emphasized investigation of things (gewu) and disciplined reflection on the classics to align the mind-heart with moral principle. His commentaries on the Four Books became orthodox for civil service examinations, shaping Confucian education for centuries. He combined metaphysical speculation with a rigorous program of ethical practice and study.
HistoricalContext: Zhu Xi lived during the Southern Song dynasty, when Confucians responded to Buddhism and Daoism by developing more explicit metaphysical systems. He held various official posts but spent much time teaching and writing in academies. Initially controversial, his interpretations were later canonized by the state, profoundly influencing China, Korea, Japan, and Vietnam until the modern era.
EpistemologicalStance: Reflective Inquiry; Textual Study; Intuitive Insight
EthicalOrientation: Virtue Ethics; Self-cultivation; Role Ethics
PoliticalOrientation: Supports Confucian Monarchy; Moral Bureaucracy
ReligiousOrientation: Confucian; Engages with Buddhist and Daoist Ideas
KeyWorks: Commentaries on the Four Books; Reflections on Things at Hand
Tags: Li and qi;Investigation of things;Orthodox Neo-Confucianism

CoreTeachings: Wang Yangming argued for the unity of knowledge and action and for innate knowing (liangzhi) of moral truth present in each person’s mind-heart. He criticized Zhu Xi’s emphasis on external investigation, stressing instead introspective realization and immediate moral responsiveness. For Wang, moral failure results from selfish desires obscuring innate moral knowledge, which can be clarified through reflection and practice in everyday life. His ideal of the sage combines inner illumination with active engagement in the world.
HistoricalContext: Wang was born into a scholar-official family in the Ming dynasty and served as both an official and military commander, putting his ideas into practice in governance and pacification campaigns. Exiled at one point for political reasons, he had a transformative experience that led to his mature philosophy. His teachings inspired later Confucian movements, especially in Japan, and were seen by some as more activist and inward than Zhu Xi’s orthodoxy.
EthicalOrientation: Virtue Ethics; Unity of Knowing and Doing
PoliticalOrientation: Supports Confucian Monarchy; Emphasizes Conscientious Officials
ReligiousOrientation: Confucian; Engages Critically with Buddhist/Daoist Ideas
KeyWorks: Instructions for Practical Living; Various Letters and Records
Tags: Unity of knowledge and action;Innate knowing;Ming Neo-Confucianism

CoreTeachings: Dōgen taught that zazen (seated meditation) is not a means to enlightenment but the very expression of Buddha-nature, encapsulated in the idea of practice-realization. He emphasized the unity of practice and everyday activity, collapsing distinctions between sacred and mundane. His reflections on time, being, and language in the Shōbōgenzō present a subtle, non-dual view of reality, where each moment fully manifests the whole. He shaped Sōtō Zen’s emphasis on monastic discipline, simplicity, and continuous practice.
HistoricalContext: Born into a noble family in Japan during the Kamakura period, Dōgen became dissatisfied with the state of Japanese Buddhism and traveled to China to study Chan (Zen). After returning, he founded the Sōtō Zen school in Japan and established the Eiheiji monastery. He wrote during a time of political transition and the rise of new Buddhist movements aimed at broader lay participation.
EthicalOrientation: Practice-centered Ethics; Monastic Discipline; Everyday Mind
PoliticalOrientation: Generally Apolitical; Focus on Monastic Community
KeyWorks: Shōbōgenzō; Eihei Kōroku; Fukanzazengi

CoreTeachings: Nāgārjuna developed the Madhyamaka philosophy of emptiness (śūnyatā), arguing that all phenomena lack inherent existence and arise only in dependence on causes, conditions, and conceptual designation. He used rigorous dialectical arguments to show that any attempt to posit intrinsic nature leads to contradictions, while conventional truth remains pragmatically valid. His two-truths doctrine distinguishes ultimate emptiness from conventional reality without positing a separate metaphysical realm. His work is central to Mahāyāna Buddhism’s understanding of wisdom and liberation.
HistoricalContext: Nāgārjuna likely lived in South India and was associated with monastic communities and early Mahāyāna movements, though historical details are sparse. Traditional accounts portray him as a monk and philosopher who corresponded with kings and religious figures. His writings spread widely across India, Tibet, China, and East Asia, becoming foundational for later Buddhist scholasticism and practice traditions.
EthicalOrientation: Bodhisattva Ethics; Middle Way
PoliticalOrientation: Generally Apolitical; Advises Rulers in Some Traditions
KeyWorks: Mūlamadhyamakakārikā; Vigrahavyāvartanī; Ratnāvalī

CoreTeachings: Śaṅkara systematized Advaita Vedānta, teaching that the ultimate reality is non-dual Brahman, pure consciousness, and that the world of multiplicity is ultimately māyā (appearance). He interpreted the Upaniṣads as revealing the identity of Ātman (self) and Brahman, attainable through knowledge (jñāna) rather than ritual works. Liberation involves realizing one’s true nature beyond body and mind, transcending ignorance. His commentaries and independent works established Advaita as a dominant school of Hindu philosophy.
HistoricalContext: Born in Kerala in South India, Śaṅkara became a renunciant monk at a young age and traveled widely, debating other schools such as Mīmāṃsā, Sāṅkhya, and various Buddhist traditions. He is credited with founding monastic centers (maṭhas) across India and revitalizing Śaiva-oriented Vedānta. His life occurred during a period of vigorous philosophical and theological contestation within Indian religious traditions.
EpistemologicalStance: Scriptural Hermeneutics; Rational Argument; Meditative Insight
EthicalOrientation: Renunciant Ethics; Knowledge-centered Liberation
PoliticalOrientation: Generally Apolitical; Supports Traditional Social-Religious Order
KeyWorks: Commentaries on the Brahma Sūtra, Upaniṣads, and Bhagavad Gītā; Upadeśasāhasrī

CoreTeachings: Rāmānuja developed Viśiṣṭādvaita (qualified non-dualism), arguing that Brahman is a personal God with attributes, and that individual souls and the world are real but dependent modes of Brahman. He criticized Śaṅkara’s strict non-dualism for downplaying devotion and the reality of difference. Emphasizing bhakti (devotion) alongside knowledge, he taught that salvation involves loving surrender to God while retaining a distinct, though dependent, self. His theology became central to major Vaiṣṇava traditions.
HistoricalContext: Rāmānuja was born in South India and became a leader within Śrīvaiṣṇava communities, associated with temple-centered devotional practices. He lived during a time when bhakti movements were reshaping Hindu religious life, making devotion more accessible across social strata. His leadership and writings helped institutionalize devotional Vedānta and temple worship, influencing religious practice in South India and beyond.
EthicalOrientation: Devotional Ethics; Bhakti and Surrender
PoliticalOrientation: Supports Temple-centered Religious Order; No Detailed Political Theory
KeyWorks: Śrībhāṣya; Gītābhāṣya; Vedārthasaṅgraha

CoreTeachings: Patañjali systematized the classical Yoga tradition, outlining an eightfold path (aṣṭāṅga yoga) that leads from ethical disciplines and bodily postures to concentration, meditation, and samādhi. He adopted a dualist metaphysics in which puruṣa (pure consciousness) is distinct from prakṛti (material nature), with liberation achieved by discriminative insight that separates the seer from the seen. The text integrates psychological analysis of mental fluctuations (citta-vṛtti) with practical techniques to still them. It became foundational for later Hindu and modern global yoga movements, even when reinterpreted.
HistoricalContext: Little is known about Patañjali as a historical figure; he likely lived in ancient India around the early centuries BCE or CE and drew on earlier yogic and Sāṃkhya ideas. The Yoga Sūtra was composed in a period of intense interaction among Hindu, Buddhist, and Jain traditions, all offering paths to liberation. Over time, commentators such as Vyāsa and later Vedāntins shaped how his terse aphorisms were understood in religious and philosophical communities.
EpistemologicalStance: Introspective Practice; Meditative Insight; Scriptural Authority
EthicalOrientation: Ascetic and Meditative Ethics; Eightfold Path of Yoga
PoliticalOrientation: Generally Apolitical; Oriented toward Individual Liberation
KeyWorks: Yoga Sūtra

CoreTeachings: The Nyāya and Vaiśeṣika traditions developed a detailed realist metaphysics and epistemology, analyzing categories such as substance, quality, motion, universals, and inherence. They articulated rigorous theories of inference, perception, comparison, and testimony as valid means of knowledge (pramāṇas). Nyāya used logical analysis to defend theism and critique rival schools, while Vaiśeṣika offered an early atomistic account of matter. Together, they provided a systematic framework for debate and proof in classical Indian philosophy.
HistoricalContext: The Nyāya-sūtra is traditionally attributed to Gautama (Akṣapāda), and the Vaiśeṣika-sūtra to Kaṇāda, active in ancient India when Brahmanical schools developed in dialogue and conflict with Buddhist and Jain thinkers. Over centuries, the two schools converged, with later commentators integrating their systems. Their debates with Buddhists, Mīmāṃsakas, Vedāntins, and others helped refine concepts of logic and ontology in South Asian thought.
EpistemologicalStance: Logical and Epistemic Analysis; Reliance on Pramāṇas
EthicalOrientation: Dharma-based Ethics; Debate on Duty and Liberation
PoliticalOrientation: Supports Traditional Social-Religious Order; No Detailed Political Theory
KeyWorks: Nyāya-sūtra; Vaiśeṣika-sūtra; Classical Commentaries

CoreTeachings: Dignāga revolutionized Indian logic and epistemology from a Buddhist standpoint, restricting valid knowledge to perception and inference. He argued that perception is non-conceptual and free from error, while conceptual construction occurs in inference and language. His analysis of inference, signs, and exclusion (apoha) provided a powerful alternative to Brahmanical theories of universals and meaning. His work laid the foundation for later Buddhist logicians and epistemologists in India and Tibet.
HistoricalContext: Dignāga lived in India during a period of flourishing Buddhist scholasticism, likely associated with monastic universities such as Nālandā. He engaged in intense polemics with Nyāya and other Hindu schools over issues of perception, universals, and language. His texts were transmitted to Tibet and East Asia, where they shaped Buddhist philosophical curricula for centuries.
EpistemologicalStance: Pramāṇa Theory; Inferential and Perceptual Analysis
EthicalOrientation: Monastic Ethics; Soteriological Orientation
PoliticalOrientation: Generally Apolitical; Focus on Scholastic Debate and Liberation
KeyWorks: Pramāṇa-samuccaya; Commentaries on Logic and Epistemology

CoreTeachings: Dharmakīrti refined and systematized Buddhist epistemology and logic, defending the reliability of pramāṇas in leading to successful action and liberation. He developed sophisticated accounts of inference, causation, and momentariness, arguing that only momentary particulars truly exist while universals are conceptual fictions. He also offered arguments for rebirth and compassion as central to Buddhist ethics. His works became central textbooks in Tibetan monastic education and influenced debates with Hindu philosophers.
HistoricalContext: Dharmakīrti lived in India several centuries after Dignāga, likely associated with major monastic centers, though biographical details are uncertain. Working in a period of intense inter-school polemic, he addressed Nyāya, Mīmāṃsā, and other rivals while building on Dignāga’s project. His influence spread beyond India to Tibet and East Asia, where his texts were extensively commented on and memorized.
MetaphysicalStance: Momentarism; Nominalism; Emptiness-compatible Realism of Particulars
EpistemologicalStance: Pramāṇa-based Rational Analysis; Soteriological Justification of Knowledge
EthicalOrientation: Bodhisattva Ethics; Compassion and Non-harm
PoliticalOrientation: Generally Apolitical; Occasional Advice to Rulers in Tradition
KeyWorks: Pramāṇavārttika; Pramāṇaviniścaya; Other Epistemological Treatises

CoreTeachings: Śāntideva articulated the Bodhisattva ideal, emphasizing the cultivation of compassion, patience, and wisdom for the benefit of all beings. In the Bodhicaryāvatāra, he combined poetic reflection and philosophical argument to show how selflessness and the understanding of emptiness transform suffering and moral life. He stressed the importance of exchanging self and other in one’s perspective to generate boundless altruism. His work remains a central text for Mahāyāna ethical practice and reflection.
HistoricalContext: According to traditional accounts, Śāntideva was a monk at Nālandā University who appeared lazy and unproductive until revealing his profound understanding through recitation of his text. Historical details are sparse, but he likely lived in a period when Mahāyāna thought and practice were well-established in Indian monastic institutions. His writings became especially influential in Tibet, shaping Buddhist ethics and contemplative practice.
EpistemologicalStance: Scriptural Reflection; Meditative Cultivation; Rational Argument
EthicalOrientation: Bodhisattva Ethics; Radical Altruism and Compassion
PoliticalOrientation: Generally Apolitical; Focus on Moral and Spiritual Transformation
KeyWorks: Bodhicaryāvatāra (Guide to the Bodhisattva’s Way of Life); Śikṣāsamuccaya

CoreTeachings: Umāsvāti systematized Jain doctrine in the Tattvārtha-sūtra, presenting a comprehensive account of reality, karma, and the path to liberation. He described multiple kinds of substances, including souls (jīva) and non-soul categories, all governed by intricate karmic laws. Emphasizing non-violence (ahiṃsā), non-attachment, and many-sidedness (anekāntavāda), he provided a framework for ethical restraint and intellectual openness. His work is accepted across major Jain sects and remains a core doctrinal text.
HistoricalContext: Umāsvāti likely lived in India during the early centuries CE, when Jainism was an established religious community alongside Hindu and Buddhist traditions. Little is known about his life, but he is associated with both Digambara and Śvetāmbara lineages, suggesting broad authority. His concise sūtras were later commented on extensively, guiding Jain monastic and lay practice over centuries.
MetaphysicalStance: Jain Pluralistic Realism; Eternal Souls and Karma
EthicalOrientation: Non-violence Ethics; Asceticism; Anekāntavāda (many-sidedness)
PoliticalOrientation: Generally Apolitical; Emphasis on Personal and Communal Conduct
KeyWorks: Tattvārtha-sūtra

CoreTeachings: Al-Ghazālī critically examined philosophy, especially Avicennan metaphysics, arguing that it fails on key theological points such as creation and divine knowledge. In The Incoherence of the Philosophers, he used rigorous argument to show the limits of reason in matters of faith while still valuing logic and philosophy for certain purposes. He also articulated a Sufi-influenced path that combines law, ethics, and inner purification, emphasizing direct experiential knowledge of God. His synthesis reshaped Sunni Islam by integrating legal, theological, and mystical dimensions.
HistoricalContext: Born in Ṭūs (in present-day Iran), al-Ghazālī studied law and theology, rising to a prestigious professorship in Baghdad under the Seljuk vizier Nizam al-Mulk. After a spiritual crisis, he left his position and spent years in retreat and travel, deepening his engagement with Sufism. Writing in a period of political fragmentation and intellectual diversity in the Islamic world, he became one of Sunni Islam’s most influential theologians and mystics.
EpistemologicalStance: Critical of Pure Philosophy; Emphasis on Revelation and Mystical Experience
EthicalOrientation: Islamic Virtue Ethics; Sufi Path of Purification
PoliticalOrientation: Supports Sunni Political Order; Critical of Corruption; No Detailed System
KeyWorks: The Incoherence of the Philosophers; The Revival of the Religious Sciences (Iḥyā’ ‘Ulūm al-Dīn); Deliverance from Error
Tags: Incoherence of the philosophers;Sufi integration;Ash‘arite theology

CoreTeachings: Ibn Khaldun developed a pioneering philosophy of history and social theory, analyzing how group solidarity (‘asabiyya) underpins the rise and fall of dynasties and states. He emphasized economic conditions, climate, and social organization as factors in political stability and decline. His work anticipates modern sociology by treating human societies as governed by discernible patterns rather than mere chance or providence. He remained a believing Muslim while offering a relatively secular, empirical account of historical processes.
HistoricalContext: Born in Tunis to an educated Andalusian family, Ibn Khaldun held various administrative and judicial posts across North Africa and Muslim Spain. He lived amid frequent dynastic changes, tribal conflicts, and shifting centers of power, experiences that informed his observations on political and social dynamics. Later in life, he served as a judge in Cairo and met Timur (Tamerlane), giving him a broad view of the Islamic world of his time.
MetaphysicalStance: Moderate Realism about Social Structures; Theistic Background
EpistemologicalStance: Empirical Observation; Rational Analysis of Historical Patterns
EthicalOrientation: Islamic Ethics; Pragmatic Advice to Rulers
PoliticalOrientation: Realist about Power; Advises Rulers; No Ideological Program
KeyWorks: Muqaddimah (Introduction to History); Kitāb al-‘Ibar

CoreTeachings: Fanon analyzed the psychological and social effects of colonialism, showing how racism and violence deform both colonizer and colonized. He argued that decolonization is inherently a violent process of creating new humans and new political orders, criticizing both assimilationist and purely nationalist solutions. Drawing on phenomenology and psychoanalysis, he examined the lived experience of Blackness under white supremacy. His work inspired anti-colonial struggles and later theories of race, identity, and liberation.
HistoricalContext: Born in Martinique, then a French colony, Fanon served in the Free French forces during World War II and later studied medicine and psychiatry in France. He worked as a psychiatrist in colonial Algeria, where he witnessed and participated in the Algerian struggle for independence. Writing in the mid-20th century amid global decolonization, he engaged with Marxism, existentialism, and anti-racist activism before dying at a young age from leukemia.
MetaphysicalStance: Historical Materialism with Existentialist and Psychoanalytic Elements
EpistemologicalStance: Phenomenological Description; Psychoanalytic Interpretation; Political Analysis
EthicalOrientation: Liberation Ethics; Anti-colonial Emancipation
PoliticalOrientation: Revolutionary; Anti-colonial; Critical of Both Colonial and Postcolonial Elites
ReligiousOrientation: Raised Catholic; Later Secular; Anti-racist Humanism
KeyWorks: Black Skin, White Masks; The Wretched of the Earth; A Dying Colonialism

CoreTeachings: Nkrumah advocated a synthesis of Marxism, African communal values, and anti-colonial nationalism, promoting what he called ‘consciencism.’ He argued that political independence is incomplete without economic liberation and social transformation, emphasizing the dangers of neocolonial control. As a political leader, he sought to build a unified, industrialized Africa rooted in socialist principles. His philosophical writings combined practical statecraft with reflections on ideology, unity, and development.
HistoricalContext: Born in the Gold Coast (now Ghana), Nkrumah studied in the United States and the United Kingdom, encountering Marxism, pan-Africanism, and anti-colonial activism. He became the first prime minister and later president of independent Ghana, spearheading infrastructure and education projects while also concentrating power. Overthrown in a coup in 1966, he spent his final years in exile, continuing to write about African unity and neocolonialism.
MetaphysicalStance: Historical Materialism Adapted to African Context; Anti-imperialist
EpistemologicalStance: Empirical and Ideological Analysis; Marxist Method
EthicalOrientation: Socialist Ethics; Pan-African Solidarity
PoliticalOrientation: Socialist; Pan-Africanist; Anti-colonial; Initially Democratic then More Authoritarian
ReligiousOrientation: Raised Catholic; Later Secular Socialist with Spiritual Language
KeyWorks: Consciencism; Neo-Colonialism: The Last Stage of Imperialism; Africa Must Unite

CoreTeachings: Senghor developed the concept of Négritude, affirming Black cultural identity and values in response to colonial denigration and assimilation. He argued that African cultures contribute distinctive sensibilities, especially in emotion, rhythm, and communalism, to a universal human civilization. As a poet and philosopher, he envisioned a synthesis of African and European traditions in a ‘civilization of the universal.’ His thought combined cultural pride with Catholic personalism and socialist leanings.
HistoricalContext: Born in Senegal under French colonial rule, Senghor studied in France and became one of the first African agrégés in grammar. He co-founded the Négritude movement with Aimé Césaire and Léon-Gontran Damas, participating in French literary and political circles. After Senegal’s independence, he served as its first president, promoting cultural policy and moderate African socialism before voluntarily resigning.
EpistemologicalStance: Hermeneutic and Poetic Reflection; Cultural Analysis
EthicalOrientation: Communitarian and Humanist Ethics; Dignity and Solidarity
PoliticalOrientation: Socialist-leaning; Moderate; Pro-French Ties; Cultural Nationalist
ReligiousOrientation: Catholic; Integrates Faith with Cultural and Political Thought
KeyWorks: Poems (Chants d’ombre, Hosties noires); On African Socialism; Essays on Négritude

CoreTeachings: Mariátegui developed a Marxism adapted to Latin American realities, emphasizing the role of Indigenous communities and land relations in Peruvian society. He rejected mechanical economic determinism, highlighting culture, myth, and revolutionary subjectivity. He saw socialism not as a copy of European models but as rooted in local histories and communal traditions. His work became foundational for Latin American Marxist and decolonial thought.
HistoricalContext: Born in Moquegua, Peru, Mariátegui left school early and worked as a journalist, later traveling to Europe where he encountered Marxist and avant-garde currents. Returning to Peru, he founded journals and political organizations, writing amid oligarchic rule, social upheaval, and Indigenous struggles. His early death at 35 cut short a promising career, but his writings continued to influence leftist movements across Latin America.
MetaphysicalStance: Historical Materialism with Cultural and Indigenous Emphasis
EpistemologicalStance: Historical and Sociological Analysis; Critical Marxist Method
EthicalOrientation: Socialist Ethics; Emphasis on Indigenous Justice and Solidarity
PoliticalOrientation: Socialist; Revolutionary; Anti-oligarchic; Anti-imperialist
ReligiousOrientation: Formally Catholic background; Later Secular Marxist with Mythic Sensibility
KeyWorks: Seven Interpretive Essays on Peruvian Reality; Defense of Marxism; Various Articles

CoreTeachings: Paz explored themes of solitude, identity, and modernity, particularly in the Mexican and broader Latin American context. He analyzed how historical traumas, colonial legacies, and cultural myths shape individual and collective self-understanding. Influenced by existentialism, surrealism, and comparative religion, he reflected on eroticism, ritual, and the sacred in modern life. His essays and poetry blend philosophical insight with literary experimentation to probe the meaning of freedom and authenticity.
HistoricalContext: Born in Mexico City, Paz grew up in a politically engaged family and became involved in leftist and literary circles. He served as a diplomat, including as ambassador to India, experiences that exposed him to diverse cultures and spiritual traditions. His life spanned the Mexican Revolution’s aftermath, the Cold War, and student protests, leading him to resign in protest after the 1968 Tlatelolco massacre.
MetaphysicalStance: Pluralistic, Poetic Ontology; Open to the Sacred
EpistemologicalStance: Hermeneutic and Poetic Reflection; Essayistic Inquiry
EthicalOrientation: Humanist Ethics; Concern with Freedom and Authenticity
PoliticalOrientation: Democratic; Initially Leftist then Critical of Dogmatic Ideologies
KeyWorks: The Labyrinth of Solitude; The Monkey Grammarian; The Double Flame

CoreTeachings: Rawls developed a theory of justice as fairness, proposing that principles of justice are those that would be chosen in an original position behind a veil of ignorance. He argued for two main principles: equal basic liberties for all and social and economic inequalities arranged to benefit the least advantaged and attached to fair equality of opportunity. His later work emphasized political, not comprehensive, liberalism, seeking stable agreement among citizens with diverse moral and religious doctrines. Rawls’s framework reshaped contemporary political philosophy and debates about distributive justice.
HistoricalContext: Born in Baltimore, Rawls served in World War II, experiences that shaped his views on morality and war. He studied at Princeton and taught at Cornell and Harvard during a period when Anglo-American philosophy was dominated by analytic methods and less focused on normative theory. His 1971 book A Theory of Justice sparked a revival of political philosophy, influencing legal theory, economics, and public policy discussions.
EthicalOrientation: Liberal Egalitarianism; Duty to Support Just Institutions
PoliticalOrientation: Liberal; Social-Democratic Leanings; Constitutional Democracy
ReligiousOrientation: Episcopal background; Later Secular; Respects Reasonable Pluralism
KeyWorks: A Theory of Justice; Political Liberalism; The Law of Peoples; Justice as Fairness: A Restatement
Tags: Veil of ignorance;Difference principle;Justice as fairness

CoreTeachings: Nozick defended a libertarian theory of justice grounded in strong individual rights to self-ownership and property acquired through just transfer. In Anarchy, State, and Utopia, he argued that any patterned distribution of holdings violates liberty, since free exchanges upset patterns, and that a minimal state limited to protection against force, theft, and fraud is justified. He criticized Rawlsian egalitarianism and utopian planning while leaving room for voluntary communities and experiments. His work became a key reference for contemporary libertarian and right-liberal thought.
HistoricalContext: Born in Brooklyn to Jewish immigrant parents, Nozick studied at Columbia and Princeton and taught at Harvard, working within the analytic philosophical tradition. Writing in the 1970s amid debates over welfare states, socialism, and market economies, he engaged directly with Rawls’s influential theory. Later in life, he wrote on knowledge, love, and value with a more eclectic, less doctrinaire tone.
EthicalOrientation: Rights-based Ethics; Self-ownership
PoliticalOrientation: Libertarian; Minimal State; Pro-market
KeyWorks: Anarchy, State, and Utopia; Philosophical Explanations; The Examined Life
Tags: Entitlement theory;Minimal state;Critique of patterned principles

CoreTeachings: MacIntyre critiqued modern moral philosophy as fragmented and emotivist, arguing that moral concepts only make sense within historical traditions and practices. He revived Aristotelian virtue ethics, emphasizing the role of practices, narratives, and communities in shaping character and goods. In After Virtue and later works, he called for local forms of community that sustain rational, tradition-constituted inquiry about the good life. His work challenges liberal individualism and supports a more historically and socially embedded ethics.
HistoricalContext: Born in Glasgow, MacIntyre was involved with various leftist and Marxist groups before turning to Thomistic-Aristotelian thought. He taught at universities in the UK and US, including Notre Dame, and wrote during a period of disillusionment with both liberalism and Marxism. His conversion to Catholicism and engagement with Aquinas shaped his mature perspective on virtue, rationality, and community.
EpistemologicalStance: Historical and Conceptual Analysis; Narrative Reasoning
EthicalOrientation: Virtue Ethics; Practice-based and Tradition-embedded Morality
PoliticalOrientation: Critical of Liberalism; Communitarian; Localist
KeyWorks: After Virtue; Whose Justice? Which Rationality?; Three Rival Versions of Moral Enquiry
Tags: Virtue ethics revival;Emotivism critique;Practices and traditions

CoreTeachings: Nussbaum developed the capabilities approach, arguing that social justice should be assessed in terms of what people are actually able to do and to be, not merely in terms of resources or utilities. Drawing on Aristotle and liberal feminism, she proposed a list of central capabilities—such as bodily health, affiliation, and practical reason—that societies should secure for all citizens. She has written extensively on emotions, literature, disability, global justice, and gender, defending a cosmopolitan, humanist ethics. Her work bridges analytic rigor with engagement in law, development, and public policy.
HistoricalContext: Born in New York City, Nussbaum studied at New York University and Harvard, working initially on ancient Greek philosophy before expanding into ethics, law, and social issues. She has taught at institutions including Brown and the University of Chicago, often collaborating with economists and legal scholars. Writing from the late 20th century onward, she has engaged debates on feminism, multiculturalism, and development, influencing both academic and policy discussions.
EpistemologicalStance: Analytic and Hermeneutic Methods; Engagement with Literature and Law
EthicalOrientation: Capabilities-based Justice; Liberal Feminist Ethics
PoliticalOrientation: Liberal; Social-democratic; Cosmopolitan
ReligiousOrientation: Raised in a secular Jewish/Christian milieu; Later Reform Jewish affiliation; Secular humanist tone
KeyWorks: Women and Human Development; Frontiers of Justice; Creating Capabilities; Upheavals of Thought

CoreTeachings: Butler argued that gender is not a fixed inner identity but is performatively constituted through repeated social acts and norms. In Gender Trouble and later works, she critiqued binary gender frameworks and essentialist notions of sex, showing how power operates through categories of intelligibility. She extended these insights to sexuality, kinship, and precarity, advocating for coalitional politics that challenge normative violence. Her work significantly influenced queer theory, feminist theory, and critical social thought.
HistoricalContext: Born in Cleveland to a Jewish family, Butler studied at Yale and has taught at institutions including UC Berkeley and Columbia. Writing from the late 20th century onward, she engaged with feminism, continental philosophy, psychoanalysis, and political theory. Her work emerged amid culture wars over gender and sexuality, LGBTQ+ activism, and debates about identity politics and universality.
MetaphysicalStance: Anti-essentialism; Constructivist Account of Gender and Subjectivity
EpistemologicalStance: Genealogical and Deconstructive Methods; Close Reading
EthicalOrientation: Ethics of Non-violence; Recognition; Precarious Life
PoliticalOrientation: Leftist; Queer and Feminist Activism; Anti-nationalist Tendencies
ReligiousOrientation: Jewish background; Secular; Engages with Jewish Ethics in Later Work
KeyWorks: Gender Trouble; Bodies That Matter; Undoing Gender; Frames of War

CoreTeachings: Taylor analyzed the modern self as shaped by historical imaginaries, moral frameworks, and narratives of authenticity. He critiqued atomistic liberalism, arguing that individual identity is dialogically formed within linguistic and cultural contexts. His work on recognition, multiculturalism, and secularization emphasized the need for societies to acknowledge diverse sources of value and meaning. He combined analytic clarity with a broad historical and phenomenological approach to modernity.
HistoricalContext: Born in Montreal, Taylor studied at McGill and Oxford and was influenced by both analytic and continental traditions. Active in Canadian politics as well as academia, he engaged debates on nationalism, bilingualism, and multiculturalism. Writing from the late 20th century onward, he has addressed issues of secularization, identity, and democracy in North America and Europe.
EthicalOrientation: Ethics of Authenticity; Recognition; Communal Goods
PoliticalOrientation: Communitarian-leaning; Social Democratic; Multiculturalist
ReligiousOrientation: Catholic; Engages Deeply with Christian Sources
KeyWorks: Sources of the Self; A Secular Age; The Ethics of Authenticity; Multiculturalism: Examining the Politics of Recognition

CoreTeachings: Nagel explored tensions between subjective experience and objective understanding, famously asking what it is like to be a bat to challenge reductionist accounts of consciousness. He argued that a complete view of reality must integrate both the personal, first-person standpoint and the impersonal, third-person ‘view from nowhere.’ In ethics and political philosophy, he examined reasons, altruism, and the demands of impartiality, often highlighting the complexity and conflict of moral perspectives. His work raises doubts about purely physicalist explanations of mind and value.
HistoricalContext: Born in Belgrade and raised in the United States, Nagel studied at Cornell, Oxford, and Harvard and taught at institutions including Princeton and NYU. Writing from the late 20th century onward, he participated in analytic debates about mind, ethics, and political theory. His critical stance on reductionism and some forms of Darwinian explanation sparked controversy while stimulating further discussion.
MetaphysicalStance: Non-reductive Realism about Mind and Value
EthicalOrientation: Deontological and Agent-relative Concerns; Complex Moral Pluralism
PoliticalOrientation: Liberal; Concerned with Justice and Legitimacy
KeyWorks: The View from Nowhere; Mortal Questions; What Does It All Mean?; Mind and Cosmos
Tags: What is it like to be a bat?;View from nowhere;Non-reductive mind

CoreTeachings: Parfit made major contributions to personal identity theory, arguing that psychological continuity and connectedness, not strict identity, ground what matters in survival. In ethics, he developed sophisticated arguments about consequentialism, deontology, and contractualism, seeking convergence among them. He also pioneered population ethics, analyzing how to compare outcomes with different numbers of people and raising puzzles like the ‘repugnant conclusion.’ His meticulous work reshaped normative ethics and metaethics in the late 20th century.
HistoricalContext: Born in China to British parents and educated at Oxford, Parfit spent most of his career at All Souls College, working largely outside the standard teaching roles. He wrote in a highly rigorous, example-driven style, influencing generations of moral philosophers. His later collaborations engaged with thinkers like Scanlon and Rawls, aiming at a more unified moral theory.
MetaphysicalStance: Non-naturalist Moral Realism; Reductionist View of Persons
EpistemologicalStance: Analytic Method; Reflective Equilibrium; Thought Experiments
EthicalOrientation: Consequentialism with Concern for Deontic and Contractualist Elements
PoliticalOrientation: Liberal; Concerned with Global Ethics and Future Generations
ReligiousOrientation: Raised in a Christian environment; Later Secular; Moral Realist
KeyWorks: Reasons and Persons; On What Matters
Tags: Personal identity reductionism;Population ethics;On What Matters

CoreTeachings: Singer defended a preference utilitarianism that extends moral concern impartially to all beings capable of suffering or enjoyment, regardless of species. He argued that failing to aid distant strangers in extreme poverty is morally comparable to ignoring a drowning child, challenging common intuitions about charity and obligation. His work on animal liberation critiqued speciesism and promoted vegetarianism or veganism as ethical requirements. He has also written on bioethics, euthanasia, and global justice, applying utilitarian reasoning to controversial practical issues.
HistoricalContext: Born in Melbourne to Austrian-Jewish refugee parents, Singer studied at the University of Melbourne and Oxford, influenced by utilitarian and analytic traditions. He has taught at institutions in Australia, the UK, and the US, including Princeton, and has been active in public debates and NGOs. Writing from the 1970s onward, he became one of the most widely known contemporary philosophers, both praised and criticized for his radical ethical conclusions.
EpistemologicalStance: Utilitarian Reasoning; Thought Experiments; Empirical Engagement
EthicalOrientation: Preference Utilitarianism; Effective Altruism; Animal Ethics
PoliticalOrientation: Left-liberal; Cosmopolitan; Supports Welfare and Animal Rights Reforms
KeyWorks: Animal Liberation; Practical Ethics; The Life You Can Save; Famine, Affluence, and Morality

CoreTeachings: West combined American pragmatism, Christianity, and Marxist social critique into a ‘prophetic pragmatism’ focused on justice, love, and democratic struggle. He analyzed race, class, and culture in the United States, emphasizing the black prophetic tradition and the need for grassroots activism. His work critiques neoliberalism, racism, and spiritual emptiness while affirming hope and solidarity among oppressed groups. As a public intellectual, he connects philosophical reflection with music, religion, and political movements.
HistoricalContext: Born in Tulsa and raised in Sacramento, West studied at Harvard and Princeton, engaging with figures like James Cone and Richard Rorty. He has taught at numerous universities, including Princeton, Harvard, and Union Theological Seminary, while remaining active in political campaigns and social movements. Writing and speaking from the late 20th century onward, he addresses civil rights, poverty, and democracy in a media-saturated, racially divided America.
MetaphysicalStance: Pragmatist and Christian Personalism; Anti-essentialist about Race
EpistemologicalStance: Hermeneutic and Pragmatist Methods; Prophetic Rhetoric
EthicalOrientation: Prophetic Ethics; Solidarity, Love, and Justice
PoliticalOrientation: Leftist; Democratic Socialist; Grassroots-oriented
KeyWorks: Race Matters; Democracy Matters; Prophesy Deliverance!

CoreTeachings: bell hooks advanced an intersectional analysis of race, gender, class, and sexuality, arguing that systems of domination are interlocking and must be challenged together. She critiqued both mainstream feminism for neglecting race and black politics for neglecting gender, emphasizing love, community, and education as sites of resistance. Her accessible writing style aimed to reach broad audiences, linking theory with everyday experience. She explored topics such as representation, pedagogy, masculinity, and healing in the context of oppression and liberation.
HistoricalContext: Born in segregated Kentucky, hooks attended segregated schools before studying at Stanford, Wisconsin, and UC Santa Cruz. She taught at various colleges and was deeply involved in activist and educational work, often writing from her experiences as a Black woman in the American South and academia. From the late 20th century onward, she became a key voice in Black feminism and critical pedagogy, influencing activists, artists, and scholars.
MetaphysicalStance: Anti-essentialist; Intersectional View of Identity and Power
EpistemologicalStance: Hermeneutic and Cultural Analysis; Narrative and Essayistic
EthicalOrientation: Intersectional Feminist Ethics; Ethics of Love and Community
PoliticalOrientation: Leftist; Anti-racist; Feminist; Grassroots-oriented
ReligiousOrientation: Raised in a Christian (Black Church) context; Spiritually engaged, critical of institutional religion
KeyWorks: Ain’t I a Woman: Black Women and Feminism; Feminist Theory: From Margin to Center; Teaching to Transgress; All About Love

CoreTeachings: The Buddha taught that human life is pervaded by suffering (duḥkha) rooted in craving and ignorance, and that liberation (nirvāṇa) is possible through understanding and transforming this condition. He articulated the Four Noble Truths and the Noble Eightfold Path as a practical framework covering right view, intention, speech, action, livelihood, effort, mindfulness, and concentration. Rejecting both eternalism and nihilism, he advanced a middle way and doctrines such as dependent origination and non-self (anātman), which challenge the idea of a permanent, independent soul. His teachings combine ethical discipline, meditative practice, and wisdom into a path aimed at the cessation of suffering.
HistoricalContext: Siddhārtha Gautama was born into a noble family in the Śākya clan in northern India (in present-day Nepal/India) during a time of social change, urbanization, and competing religious movements. According to traditional accounts, he left a sheltered palace life to become an ascetic, eventually attaining awakening under the Bodhi tree after years of intense practice and experimentation. He then spent decades traveling and teaching across the Ganges plain, founding a monastic community (saṅgha) whose oral transmission of his discourses formed the basis of early Buddhist canons.
EpistemologicalStance: Empirical-Experiential Inquiry; Meditative Insight; Pragmatic Verification
EthicalOrientation: Virtue and Compassion-based Ethics; Middle Way; Monastic and Lay Codes
PoliticalOrientation: Generally Apolitical; Offers Advice to Rulers; Favors Ethical Kingship
ReligiousOrientation: Buddhist (Founder of Buddhism; Non-theistic in Metaphysical Outlook)
KeyWorks: Pāli Nikāyas (e.g., Dīgha, Majjhima, Saṃyutta, Aṅguttara); Vinaya Piṭaka; Early Sūtras
Tags: Four Noble Truths;Eightfold Path;Anātman and dependent origination
